skill.name: routeros-qemu-chr
skill.description: Comprehensive guide for running MikroTik RouterOS CHR (Cloud Hosted Router) in QEMU virtualization. Covers image download, boot configuration (SeaBIOS/UEFI), VirtIO device setup, acceleration detection (KVM/HVF/TCG), port forwarding, and CI/CD integration.
skill.summary: Complete documentation for running MikroTik RouterOS CHR in QEMU virtualization across x86_64 and aarch64 architectures.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 303 potential security issues in code examples and documentation. All findings are false positives: external commands are legitimate QEMU invocations for virtualization, network operations access official MikroTik servers, and filesystem operations are standard QEMU configuration patterns. No malicious intent detected.

content.value_statement: Running MikroTik RouterOS CHR in QEMU is complex due to architecture-specific boot methods and VirtIO driver limitations. This skill provides tested patterns for x86_64 and aarch64, covering boot configuration, acceleration detection, and CI/CD integration.
actual_capabilities: Generate QEMU command lines for x86_64 and aarch64 RouterOS CHR virtual machines
Detect and configure hardware acceleration (KVM on Linux, HVF on macOS, TCG fallback)
Configure VirtIO devices correctly for RouterOS kernel driver compatibility
Set up port forwarding for RouterOS services (WebFig, REST API, SSH, WinBox)
Implement health check patterns to wait for CHR boot completion
Create GitHub Actions workflows for CI/CD testing with RouterOS
limitations: Direct kernel boot does not work for RouterOS CHR on either architecture
Cross-architecture TCG emulation (x86_64 on ARM hosts) is too slow for practical use
The check-installation REST API call fails on aarch64 due to firmware limitations
No virtio-mmio driver support requires explicit PCI device specification on aarch64

use_cases.title: CI/CD Schema Testing
use_cases.description: Automatically generate RouterOS configuration schemas in GitHub Actions by running CHR in QEMU and querying the REST API

use_cases.title: Local Development Environment
use_cases.description: Quickly spin up isolated RouterOS instances for testing firewall rules, scripts, and configuration changes without physical hardware

use_cases.title: Multi-Architecture Testing
use_cases.description: Validate RouterOS configurations and packages on both x86_64 and aarch64 virtual machines before deployment to mixed infrastructure

prompt_templates.title: Start QEMU CHR with Basic Networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a QEMU command to start RouterOS CHR x86_64 with KVM acceleration, 256MB RAM, and forward port 9180 to the guest HTTP port
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick local testing with WebFig access

prompt_templates.title: Configure aarch64 CHR with UEFI
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete QEMU launch command for RouterOS CHR aarch64 using EDK2 UEFI firmware, VirtIO PCI devices, and HVF acceleration on macOS

prompt_templates.title: GitHub Actions Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow that downloads CHR image, installs QEMU with KVM, launches the VM, waits for boot via HTTP health check, and queries the REST API
prompt_templates.scenario: Automated CI/CD pipeline for RouterOS testing

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Instance Port Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a QEMU command pattern that runs multiple CHR instances simultaneously with unique host port prefixes (9180, 9181, 9182) for REST API, SSH, and API-SSL forwarding
prompt_templates.scenario: Parallel integration testing with isolated RouterOS instances

output_examples.input: Generate a QEMU command for CHR x86_64 with KVM and REST API port forwarding
output_examples.output: qemu-system-x86_64 -M q35 -m 256 -smp 1 -enable-kvm -drive file=chr-7.22.img,format=raw,if=virtio -netdev user,id=net0,hostfwd=tcp::9180-:80 -device virtio-net-pci,netdev=net0 -display none -serial stdio

output_examples.input: Create a TypeScript health check function for RouterOS boot
output_examples.output: async function waitForBoot(url: string, timeoutMs = 60000): Promise<boolean> { const deadline = Date.now() + timeoutMs; while (Date.now() < deadline) { try { const r = await fetch(url, { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(2000) }); if (r.ok) return true; } catch { } await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 2000)); } return false; }

output_examples.input: Show the correct aarch64 QEMU command with VirtIO PCI devices
output_examples.output: qemu-system-aarch64 -M virt -cpu cortex-a710 -m 256 -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,unit=0,file=edk2-aarch64-code.fd -drive if=pflash,format=raw,unit=1,file=my-vars.fd -drive file=chr-arm64.img,format=raw,if=none,id=drive0 -device virtio-blk-pci,drive=drive0 -netdev user,id=net0,hostfwd=tcp::9180-:80 -device virtio-net-pci,netdev=net0 -display none -serial stdio

best_practices: Always use explicit -device virtio-blk-pci on aarch64 instead of if=virtio shorthand to avoid MMIO incompatibility
Implement health checks using HTTP GET to port 80 instead of waiting for fixed durations
Use unique host port prefixes when running multiple CHR instances to prevent port conflicts
anti_patterns: Do not use -kernel direct boot on any architecture as RouterOS requires full firmware boot path
Avoid cross-architecture TCG emulation of x86_64 on ARM hosts due to excessive I/O port overhead
Never skip architecture matching checks for KVM and HVF acceleration as both require host/guest alignment

faq.question: Why does CHR fail to boot on aarch64 when I use if=virtio?
faq.answer: The if=virtio shorthand resolves to virtio-mmio on aarch64 virt machines, but RouterOS kernel only has virtio-pci driver. Always use explicit -device virtio-blk-pci syntax.

faq.question: Can I run CHR x86_64 on my Apple Silicon Mac?
faq.answer: Technically yes via TCG emulation, but boot times exceed 5 minutes making it impractical. Run aarch64 CHR natively with HVF for 20-second boot times.

faq.question: How do I know when CHR has finished booting?
faq.answer: Poll HTTP port 80 with curl or fetch. RouterOS WebFig returns HTTP 200 without authentication, making it ideal for health checks.

faq.question: Why does check-installation fail on aarch64?
faq.answer: QEMU virt machine with ACPI generates empty DTB, so RouterOS cannot find hardware capability files. This is unresolvable; skip the check on aarch64.

faq.question: What is the difference between SeaBIOS and UEFI boot?
faq.answer: Standard CHR images use proprietary boot partition that only SeaBIOS can read via MBR. UEFI boot requires repackaged fat-chr image with standard FAT16 EFI partition.

faq.question: Can I use VirtIO for filesystem sharing between host and guest?
faq.answer: No, while x86_64 kernel has 9p VirtIO driver, RouterOS does not expose mount commands. Use /tool/fetch, SCP, or FTP for file transfers instead.
